question: In Exodus 13, every firstborn belongs to God.

memoryAid.text: Because the Lord spared Israel's firstborn at the Passover, every firstborn is consecrated to Him, set apart and given to God.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: How many days did the Feast of Unleavened Bread last?

memoryAid.text: Seven days, a full and perfect week of unleavened bread — seven for completeness.

question: God led Israel by the shortest route to Canaan.

memoryAid.text: Picture God steering the people away from the coastal road of the Philistines, lest they see war, lose heart, and turn back to Egypt — the longer desert road keeps a freed but untested people from running home.

question: By what did the Lord lead Israel during the day?

memoryAid.text: Cloud for cover by day, fire for light by night — shade when the sun is up, flame when it is dark.

question: By what did the Lord lead Israel during the night?

memoryAid.text: The pillar of cloud by day and fire by night is the glory and presence of the Lord, guiding His people through the wilderness.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: What did Moses bring with him out of Egypt, fulfilling an ancient promise?

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses carrying a centuries-old coffin out of Egypt on the night of flight — Joseph made them swear to carry his bones home, and now those bones ride to the promised land at last.

question: Whose promise was fulfilled when Moses carried bones out of Egypt?
answer: Joseph's, that he would reach the promised land
options: Joseph's, that he would reach the promised land
Abraham's, that he would be buried in Canaan
Jacob's, that he would see Egypt again
Levi's, that his tribe would serve the altar

memoryAid.text: Joseph's bones, Joseph's promise — the man who saved Egypt finally goes home to Canaan.

question: Where did Israel camp before the sea?
answer: Opposite Baal Zephon, between Migdol and the sea
options: Opposite Baal Zephon, between Migdol and the sea
At Elim among the springs and palms
At Marah by the bitter waters

memoryAid.text: Migdol on one side, the sea on the other, Baal Zephon ahead — boxed in, with nowhere to go but through the water.

question: Israel camped opposite Baal Zephon, between the sea and what fortified place?

memoryAid.text: Migdol means 'tower' — a tower marks the trap that turns into deliverance.

question: Israel was confident when Pharaoh approached at the sea.

memoryAid.text: Two million people pinned against the water, dust rising from Pharaoh's chariots behind them — they cried out in terror, not confidence.

question: Complete the verse: 'The Lord will fight for you, and you shall hold your ____.'

memoryAid.text: Two million terrified people, sea ahead, chariots behind — and the command is to be still. Picture standing dead-calm with hooves thundering at your back: salvation is God's work, not yours.

question: According to the OSB, by what kind of wind was the sea divided?

memoryAid.text: The OSB reads that a strong south wind (LXX) drove back the sea. The crossing of the Red Sea is a type of Baptism: Israel passes through the waters from slavery into freedom, baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea, as we are delivered through the font.
memoryAid.source: 1 Cor 10:1-2; Theodoret of Cyrus, Questions on Exodus

question: The Egyptians crossed the sea safely behind Israel.

memoryAid.text: The waters returned and covered Pharaoh's host so that not one remained. The drowning of the Egyptians figures the baptismal laver: the Egyptians are the sins drowned, Pharaoh is the devil, and Israel passes through unharmed.
memoryAid.source: Theodoret of Cyrus, Questions on Exodus

question: What happened to Pharaoh's army at the Red Sea?
answer: The chariots and horsemen perished in the returning waters
options: The chariots and horsemen perished in the returning waters
They turned back to Egypt unharmed
They crossed and pursued Israel into the wilderness
They were struck blind and wandered off

memoryAid.text: Picture chariot wheels jamming in the sea-floor mud as the walls of water collapse — horse and rider, chariot and charioteer, all swept under in an instant.

question: Complete the opening of the Song of Moses: 'Let us sing to the Lord, for he is very greatly glorified: horse and rider he has thrown into the ____.'

memoryAid.text: The Song of Moses celebrates the victory of God over the powers of Egypt at the dawn of Israel's salvation, and is sung in the Church as the first of the biblical odes.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Complete the verse: 'He was to me a helper and protector for salvation: this is my God, and I will ____ him.'

memoryAid.text: Helper, Protector, Salvation — then 'glorify': God acts, and we respond with glory.

question: Complete the verse: 'The Lord bringing wars to nought, the Lord is his ____.'

memoryAid.text: The Lord brings wars to nought — His very name spells the end of war.

question: What title is given to Miriam in the Song of Moses?

memoryAid.text: Miriam the prophetess — Moses' sister speaks and sings for God.

question: Miriam led the women with dance and what instrument?

memoryAid.text: Picture Miriam at the head of a line of women, timbrels ringing and feet dancing on the far shore while Egypt lies drowned behind them.

question: The people found fresh, sweet water when they arrived at Marah.

memoryAid.text: Marah means 'bitter' — the name itself tells you the water could not be drunk.

question: What did Moses cast into the bitter waters of Marah to make them sweet?

memoryAid.text: The Fathers see the Cross at Marah: bitter water made sweet by a tree cast in, as the bitterness of death is sweetened by the wood of the Cross.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: Complete the verse: 'I am the Lord thy God that ____ thee.'

memoryAid.text: At Marah the Lord reveals Himself as the One who heals, sweetening the bitter water by the wood — which the Fathers read as the wood of the Cross that heals the bitterness of fallen human nature.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: How many springs of water did Israel find at the oasis of Elim?

memoryAid.text: Twelve springs and seventy palm trees at Elim — twelve like the Apostles, seventy like the disciples Christ sent out, an oasis of refreshment after Marah.

question: How many palm trees were at the oasis of Elim?

memoryAid.text: Twelve springs, seventy palms — twelve like the Apostles, seventy like the disciples Christ sent out.

question: At Elim Israel found twelve springs of water and how many palm trees?

memoryAid.text: Picture a green island in the desert: twelve gushing springs and seventy palm trees swaying — a resting place after the bitterness of Marah.

question: On what day did Israel come to the Wilderness of Sin?
answer: The fifteenth day of the second month
options: The fifteenth day of the second month
The first day of the first month
The fortieth day after the Passover
The seventh day of the third month

memoryAid.text: Fifteenth of the second month — exactly one month after leaving Egypt.

question: Israel reached the Wilderness of Sin about one month after leaving Egypt.

memoryAid.text: Left Egypt on the fifteenth, hungry in the wilderness on the fifteenth a month later — one month to the day.

question: Israel journeyed from Elim into which wilderness, where the manna was given?

memoryAid.text: From Elim's springs to the Wilderness of Sin — the place-name 'Sin' is from Sinai, not the word for transgression.

question: What did the people say they had sat beside in Egypt when they complained of hunger?

memoryAid.text: Picture freed slaves longing for the steaming meat-pots of their captivity — they would rather have died full in Egypt than walk free and hungry in the desert.

question: In chapter 16 Israel went from singing God's praises to complaining about hunger.

memoryAid.text: Picture the same crowd that sang in triumph on the far shore now grumbling over empty stomachs only weeks later — from the Song of the Sea to murmuring over bread, the fickleness of the human heart laid bare.

question: When did the manna appear each day?

memoryAid.text: Bread in the morning, meat at night — manna with the dew, quail with the dusk.

question: When did the quail appear?

memoryAid.text: Quail in the evening, manna in the morning — flesh by night, bread by day.

question: What does the word 'manna' mean?

memoryAid.text: Seeing the strange bread, Israel asked 'What is it?' — and that question became its name. Christ takes up this manna as a sign of Himself, the true bread from heaven, the Bread of Life.

question: Israel could store leftover manna for the next day without it spoiling.

memoryAid.text: Manna hoarded overnight bred worms and stank — except the Sabbath portion. Gather fresh each day: it teaches daily dependence, 'give us this day our daily bread.'

question: How much manna did Israel gather on the sixth day?
options: The same as every other day

memoryAid.text: Sixth day, double portion — gather twice so you can rest on the seventh.

question: According to Exodus 16:8, against whom was Israel's murmuring truly directed?

memoryAid.text: Picture the people shaking their fists at Moses and Aaron, while Moses answers that the complaint sails right past them — their murmuring is aimed at the Lord who fed them with bread from heaven.

question: According to Exodus 16:8, the Lord gives flesh in the evening and what in the morning?

memoryAid.text: Flesh in the evening, bread in the morning — evening meat, morning bread.

question: How much manna was each person to gather according to Exodus 16:16?

memoryAid.text: A homer for each head — one measure per person, no more, no less.

question: According to Exodus 16:31, what was the taste of the manna like?

memoryAid.text: White like coriander seed and tasting like a wafer made with honey, the manna points to Christ the Bread of Life, the true bread come down from heaven.

question: Manna was white and like the seed of what plant in appearance?

memoryAid.text: Picture the desert floor at dawn dusted with tiny white seeds like coriander, glittering with the morning dew — bread from heaven scattered on the ground.

question: For how many years did Israel eat manna?

memoryAid.text: Forty years of wandering, forty years of manna — bread from heaven the whole way.

question: Israel ate manna for forty years until they reached the borders of Canaan.

memoryAid.text: Manna fed Israel for forty years until they entered the promised land — the same spiritual food the Apostle recalls, fulfilled in Christ the Bread of Life who sustains the new Israel on her pilgrimage.

question: Which of these is NOT something that happened in the Wilderness of Sin?
answer: Water flowed from a struck rock
options: Water flowed from a struck rock
A double portion was gathered on the sixth day

memoryAid.text: Chapter 16 is bread and quail; the rock that gives water comes later, in chapter 17.
